summary: Ancient Greek tragedian (~480–406 BCE). Most commonly identified by Medea, The Bacchae, The Trojan Women, Hippolytus, and his appearances as a character in Aristophanes' comedies (The Frogs, Thesmophoriazusae, The Acharnians). In The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides' plays inferior to Aeschylus's. Supposedly died in Macedonia after self-imposed exile; legend says torn apart by Molossian hounds.

works.name: As Character in Aristophanes
works.description: Euripides appears frequently as a character in Aristophanes' comedies, particularly The Frogs, Thesmophoriazusae, and The Acharnians.

clues.clue: In The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides inferior to Aeschylus in a poetry contest — Euripides's words are lighter on a literal scale
examples: Dionysus judges this man's plays to be inferior to those of Aeschylus
his descriptions of the Argo, persuasion, and an iron club do not weigh as much as the words of his opponent

clues.clue: In The Frogs, Aeschylus interrupts Euripides' prologues by shouting 'Lost his little flask of oil!'
examples: keeps saying that this man 'lost his little can of oil' as he tries to recite the prologues
is repeatedly interrupted by a rival who shouts, 'Lost his little flask of oil!'

clues.clue: In The Acharnians, Dicaeopolis borrows a beggar's costume from Euripides (based on Telephus); given rags, cap, staff, chipped cup
examples: goes to this man's house and exasperates him by requesting a beggar's outfit and some vegetables
agrees to give away a beggar's rags, cap, and staff, a chipped cup, and a pot with a sponge

clues.clue: In Thesmophoriazusae, Euripides convinces his relative Mnesilochus to spy on the women-only Thesmophoria festival
examples: invents this man's fictional relative Mnesilochus, with whom he crashes the women-only Thesmophoria festival
This character convinces Mnesilochus to spy on a women's-only festival

clues.clue: Slave says 'he is and he isn't' when asked if Euripides is home (Acharnians); addresses prayer to 'ether, tongue, nose, and wit' (Frogs)
examples: When a man asks whether this character is home, a slave answers that 'he is and he isn't'
addresses a prayer to the ether, his tongue, his nose, and his wit

works.name: Medea
works.description: Tragedy in which Medea kills her children and Glauce after being abandoned by Jason. She escapes on a divine chariot pulled by dragons.

clues.clue: Medea kills her children after being abandoned by Jason; escapes on a golden chariot pulled by dragons
examples: ends as the title character flees on a divine chariot pulled by dragons
the title character kills her and Jason's children

clues.clue: Messenger recounts Glauce's death via a poisoned robe; chorus of Corinthian women
examples: A chorus of Corinthian women features in this play, in which a messenger recounts Glauce's death via a poisoned robe

clues.clue: Medea says she'd 'rather stand three times to face their battles, shield in hand, than bear one child'
examples: says that she would rather stand 'three times to face their battles, shield in hand, than bear one child!'

clues.clue: Only Ancient Greek play where a character guilty of killing a family member goes unpunished
examples: this only Ancient Greek play in which a character guilty of killing a family member goes unpunished

clues.clue: Adapted by Luis Alfaro (Mojada — Medea as seamstress crossing US-Mexico border) and Cherríe Moraga (The Hungry Woman)
examples: Luis Alfaro's adaptation of this play
adapted into Cherrie Moraga's The Hungry Woman

works.name: The Bacchae
works.description: Tragedy about Pentheus and the worship of Dionysus. Agave kills her own son Pentheus in a Bacchic frenzy.

clues.clue: Pentheus convinced to crossdress to spy on women (Bacchants) who draw milk and honey from ground with thyrsi
examples: a king is convinced to crossdress in order to spy on a group of women who had used their thyrsi to draw milk and honey from the ground

clues.clue: Agave believes she's ripped a mountain lion to pieces but it's actually her son Pentheus
examples: a woman believes she has ripped a mountain lion to pieces, but realizes it was actually her son
Agave kills her own son

clues.clue: Wole Soyinka adapted it — set amid civil unrest in Nigeria; Pentheus's head leaks blood transformed into wine for a 'Communion Rite'
examples: Wole Soyinka's adaptation of The Bacchae, which is set amid civil unrest in Nigeria

works.name: The Trojan Women
works.description: Tragedy about the fate of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra, and Helen after the fall of Troy. Commentary on the Athenian sacking of Milos.

clues.clue: Describes fate of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra, and Helen after Troy's fall; Andromache's son Astyanax is killed
examples: describes the fate of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra and Helen
Talthybius carries onto the stage the corpse of Astyanax

clues.clue: Commentary on the Athenian sacking of Milos during the Peloponnesian War
examples: considered a commentary on the Athenian sacking of Milos

clues.clue: Femi Osofisan adapted it for the Owu Kingdom; Anne Carson created a comic book adaptation with Rosanna Bruno
examples: Femi Osofisan created the characters Anlugbua and Lawumi in a play set in the Owu Kingdom
Anne Carson collaborated with Rosanna Bruno to create a comic book adaptation

works.name: Other Plays
works.description: Other plays by Euripides mentioned in the clues.

clues.clue: Hippolytus — basis for Racine's Phèdre
examples: Racine's Phedre was based on this ancient Greek writer's play Hippolytus

clues.clue: Orestes — penultimate act ends after title character spares a Phrygian eunuch; Apollo arrives as deus ex machina; says 'We are slaves to the gods'
examples: In 2017, British classicist Armand D'Angour reconstructed the chorus from a papyrus of this play
tells his uncle 'We are slaves to the gods. Whatever gods are'

clues.clue: Alcestis — adapted by Efua Sutherland as Edufa (set in Ghana)
examples: wrote the play Edufa based on Euripides' play Alcestis

clues.clue: Ion — tragicomedy set at Delphi
examples: this author's tragicomedy Ion

clues.clue: The Phoenician Women — war between Polyneices and Eteocles prevents pilgrims from reaching Delphi
examples: The war between Polyneices and Eteocles prevents a group of pilgrims from making it to Delphi

clues.clue: Heracles — title character kills Lycus then murders his own wife and children; Anne Carson adapted it as H of H Playbook
examples: he kills Lycus for trying to murder four characters, only to murder them himself
Carson's H of H Playbook adapts this Greek author's play Heracles

clues.clue: Ancient Greek tragedian; supposedly went to live in a cave after two bad marriages
examples: supposedly went to live in a cave after two disastrous marriages

clues.clue: Died in Macedonia — patron Archelaus offered him patronage; torn apart by Molossian hounds according to legend
examples: offered patronage to Agathon and Euripides, both of whom moved to his court and died there
this writer was torn apart by Molossian hounds while in self-imposed exile in Macedonia

clues.clue: Plutarch: his verses persuaded Spartans not to destroy Athens
examples: Plutarch relates that his verses persuaded victorious Spartans not to destroy Athens

comprehensive_summary:
Euripides (~480–406 BCE) was an ancient Greek tragedian, one of the three great Athenian tragic dramatists alongside Aeschylus and Sophocles. He is most frequently clued through his appearances as a character in Aristophanes' comedies (The Frogs, The Acharnians, Thesmophoriazusae) and through his plays Medea, The Bacchae, The Trojan Women, Hippolytus, and Orestes.

In Aristophanes' The Frogs, Dionysus judges Euripides inferior to Aeschylus in a poetry contest, where a literal scale shows Euripides' descriptions of the Argo and 'iron club' weigh less than Aeschylus's 'two chariots and two corpses.' Aeschylus interrupts Euripides' prologues by shouting 'Lost his little flask of oil!' In The Acharnians, Dicaeopolis borrows a beggar's costume (from the lost play Telephus) from Euripides. In Thesmophoriazusae, Euripides convinces his relative Mnesilochus to spy on the women-only Thesmophoria festival.

Medea is his most famous play: Medea kills her children after Jason abandons her, has Glauce killed via a poisoned robe, and escapes on a golden chariot pulled by dragons. She says she'd 'rather stand three times to face their battles, shield in hand, than bear one child.' It is the only Greek play where a family murderer goes unpunished. Modern adaptations include Luis Alfaro's Mojada and Cherríe Moraga's The Hungry Woman.

In The Bacchae, Pentheus is convinced to crossdress to spy on women who draw milk and honey from the ground; his mother Agave kills him in a Bacchic frenzy, believing she's ripping apart a mountain lion. Wole Soyinka adapted it for Nigeria. The Trojan Women describes the fates of Hecuba, Andromache, Cassandra, and Helen; Astyanax is killed. It comments on Athens' sacking of Milos.

Other plays: Hippolytus (basis for Racine's Phèdre), Orestes (with its surviving musical fragment), Heracles (adapted by Anne Carson as H of H Playbook), Ion (tragicomedy at Delphi), The Phoenician Women, and Alcestis (adapted by Efua Sutherland). He supposedly died in Macedonia at Archelaus's court, torn apart by Molossian hounds. Plutarch says his verses persuaded Spartans not to destroy Athens.
recursive_suggestions: Medea — has its own answerline with many tossups

The Frogs (Aristophanes) — major source of Euripides-as-character clues
